title: A Study in Scarlet
synopsis: Watson, recently returned from Afghanistan, meets Sherlock Holmes and agrees to share lodgings at 221B Baker Street. Holmes investigates the murder of Enoch Drebber, found dead in an empty house with the word 'RACHE' written in blood on the wall. Holmes deduces the crime was committed by Jefferson Hope, a cab driver seeking vengeance for the deaths of his beloved Lucy Ferrier and her father at the hands of Mormon leaders in Utah.
solution: Enoch Drebber and his secretary were murdered by Jefferson Hope, an American cab driver avenging the death of Lucy Ferrier. Hope forced Drebber to choose one of two pills — one poison, one harmless — as an act of divine justice. Hope is apprehended but dies of an aortic aneurysm before trial.
notable_elements: First appearance of Sherlock Holmes and Watson
First appearance of Lestrade and Gregson
Holmes and Watson establish their partnership at 221B Baker Street
The 'RACHE' clue — German for 'revenge'
Novel structure: Part 1 in London, Part 2 in Utah (backstory)

title: The Sign of the Four
synopsis: Mary Morstan enlists Holmes after receiving a series of mysterious pearls and a note summoning her to a secret meeting. Holmes and Watson accompany her and learn of the Agra treasure — a vast hoard of Indian jewels linked to a pact between four convicts. When their contact Bartholomew Sholto is found murdered and the treasure stolen, Holmes pursues the killer across the Thames in a famous steam-launch chase.
solution: Jonathan Small, one of the four original conspirators, committed the murder with the help of his Andaman Islander companion Tonga. Small is captured on the Thames but throws the Agra treasure into the river rather than let it fall to the Sholto family. Watson and Mary Morstan become engaged in the aftermath.
notable_elements: Introduction of Mary Morstan, who becomes Watson's wife
The Agra treasure — jewels from an Indian mutiny-era heist
Famous Thames steam-launch chase
Holmes's cocaine use addressed directly by Watson
Jonathan Small's backstory told in a lengthy confession spanning two chapters

title: A Scandal in Bohemia
synopsis: The King of Bohemia hires Holmes to recover a compromising photograph held by Irene Adler. Holmes investigates and stages a fake fire at Adler's home to discover where she has hidden it — but Adler has recognized Holmes's disguise and departs England with her husband before the photograph can be recovered.
solution: Irene Adler outmaneuvers Holmes entirely. She identifies him despite his disguise, leaves England with her husband, and keeps the photograph — but assures the King she has no intention of using it. Holmes asks for and receives only a photograph of Adler as payment.
notable_elements: First and only major appearance of Irene Adler
The only case in which Holmes is decisively outsmarted
Origin of Holmes referring to Adler as 'The Woman'
One of the few cases where Holmes does not solve the problem he was hired to solve

title: The Red-Headed League
synopsis: Jabez Wilson, a red-headed pawnbroker, consults Holmes about a peculiar organization called the Red-Headed League, which paid him handsomely to copy out the Encyclopaedia Britannica in a Fleet Street office — until it dissolved overnight without explanation. Holmes quickly recognizes the scheme as a ruse designed to keep Wilson away from his shop while his assistant and accomplices tunnel into a nearby bank vault.
solution: John Clay and his confederate used the Red-Headed League to occupy Wilson and dig a tunnel from his cellar into the vault of the City and Suburban Bank. Holmes ambushes them in the vault and Clay is arrested, while his confederate escapes. Holmes notes that Clay is the grandson of a royal duke.
notable_elements: One of the most celebrated Holmes short stories
The elaborate deception of the Red-Headed League as a distraction from a bank robbery
Holmes deduces the scheme purely from the details of Wilson's account
Features Holmes's love of music — he attends a Sarasate concert between deductions
Introduction of John Clay, a gentleman-criminal

title: A Case of Identity
synopsis: Mary Sutherland consults Holmes about the mysterious disappearance of her fiancé Hosmer Angel, who vanished on their wedding morning. Holmes discerns almost immediately that the courtship and disappearance are entirely contrived, but listens patiently to the full account before revealing his conclusion.
solution: Hosmer Angel does not exist — he is Mary's stepfather James Windibank in heavy disguise. Windibank invented the suitor to keep Mary and her income under his control, then staged the disappearance to prevent her from forming any real attachment. Holmes exposes the scheme but cannot legally charge Windibank, whom he sends away with a threat of horsewhipping.
notable_elements: Holmes solves the case entirely by reasoning, without leaving Baker Street
The villain goes unpunished — one of the few cases with no legal resolution
Holmes deliberately withholds the solution from the client to spare her feelings
Features Holmes's typewriter-analysis deduction identifying disguised correspondence

title: The Boscombe Valley Mystery
synopsis: Holmes and Watson travel to Herefordshire to investigate the murder of Charles McCarthy near Boscombe Pool. All circumstantial evidence points to his son James, who was seen quarreling with the victim shortly before his death. Holmes suspects a deeper secret from the men's Australian past lies behind the killing.
solution: John Turner, a wealthy landowner, killed McCarthy to protect his daughter from a blackmail scheme. Turner had been a highway robber in Australia known as Black Jack of Ballarat, and McCarthy had recognized him and extorted money for years. Holmes suppresses the truth and secures James McCarthy's acquittal, allowing the dying Turner to live out his remaining days in peace.
notable_elements: Holmes clears a wrongly accused man through evidence rather than confession
Compassionate resolution — Holmes withholds the truth to spare an innocent family
The clue of the 'Cooee' call and Australian connections
One of the earliest examples of Holmes's flexible approach to justice over strict law

title: The Five Orange Pips
synopsis: Young John Openshaw consults Holmes in a storm-battered evening, reporting that members of his family have died shortly after receiving envelopes containing five orange pips and initials K.K.K. Holmes deduces the threat comes from the Ku Klux Klan, whose papers Openshaw's uncle had brought back from America.
solution: The Ku Klux Klan sent the death warnings demanding return of certain incriminating papers. Despite Holmes's warning, Openshaw is murdered before Holmes can protect him. Holmes sends his own message to the Klan's ship captain, and learns later that the vessel sank in the Atlantic — Holmes regards this as justice delivered by fate.
notable_elements: One of the rare cases where Holmes fails to save the client
The Ku Klux Klan as villains — an unusually political story
Holmes's personal fury at Openshaw's murder
Tragic ending with no formal legal resolution

title: The Man with the Twisted Lip
case_nickname: The Man with the Twisted Lip
synopsis: Watson is called to retrieve a friend from an opium den and encounters Holmes there in disguise, investigating the disappearance of Neville St. Clair. St. Clair was last seen at a window above a beggar's den in Upper Swandam Lane; his coat was found in the Thames weighted with coins, but his body was never recovered.
solution: Neville St. Clair and the disfigured beggar Hugh Boone are the same person. St. Clair had discovered years ago that he could earn far more as a professional beggar than as a journalist, and has lived a double life ever since. He is found alive, having retreated into his beggar persona to avoid being seen — his wife agrees to keep the secret.
notable_elements: Holmes in disguise in an opium den
The twist: no crime has been committed — the missing man chose his disappearance
Victorian commentary on poverty, disguise, and social class
Watson's famous confusion of Holmes in disguise among the opium smokers

title: The Adventure of the Blue Carbuncle
synopsis: Shortly after Christmas, a commissionaire brings Holmes a battered hat and a Christmas goose that he found after breaking up a street scuffle. Holmes deduces the hat's owner's history, then discovers the Countess of Morcar's stolen blue carbuncle gem lodged inside the goose. Holmes traces the gem back through a chain of geese to a plumber named Henry Baker and ultimately to the thief.
solution: James Ryder, a hotel attendant, stole the blue carbuncle and hid it in the goose to smuggle it out. He had been tipped off by Catherine Cusack, the countess's maid, and framed a plumber for the theft. Holmes allows Ryder to go free rather than have him prosecuted, reasoning that punishment would ruin him while the chance of freedom might redeem him.
notable_elements: Set at Christmas — one of the most festive Holmes stories
The Blue Carbuncle gem — one of the canon's most famous MacGuffins
Holmes exercises mercy rather than strict justice — allows the guilty man to flee
Chain-of-custody detective work tracing the gem through a series of geese

title: The Adventure of the Speckled Band
synopsis: Helen Stoner comes to Baker Street in fear for her life: her twin sister Julia died mysteriously on the eve of her wedding, whispering of a speckled band, and now Helen is being moved into the same room where Julia died. Their stepfather Dr. Grimesby Roylott — a violent, financially motivated man — keeps exotic animals on the estate. Holmes investigates the room and waits in the dark.
solution: Roylott trained a venomous Indian swamp adder to travel through a ventilator, down a bell-rope, and bite whoever slept in the bed below. When Holmes drives the snake back through the ventilator, it turns on Roylott and kills him. Holmes acknowledges he is indirectly responsible for Roylott's death but feels no remorse.
notable_elements: Widely cited as Doyle's favourite Holmes story and a fan favourite
The snake as murder weapon — ingeniously undetectable in the original crime
Dr. Roylott as one of the most physically menacing Holmes villains
Holmes and Watson spend the night in darkness waiting for the attack
Holmes bends a fireplace poker with his bare hands to show his own strength

title: The Adventure of the Engineer's Thumb
synopsis: Watson brings a new client to Holmes: hydraulic engineer Victor Hatherley, who has just had his thumb severed and escaped from a remote country house. Hatherley was hired at suspiciously high pay to repair a hydraulic press, only to discover it was part of a counterfeiting operation. When he threatened to expose them, the gang tried to kill him using the press itself.
solution: The gang was using a large hydraulic press to coin counterfeit currency. Colonel Lysander Stark led the operation, assisted by a German confederate. They attempt to crush Hatherley in the press, but a woman in the house helps him escape through a window — at the cost of his thumb. The gang escapes and the house burns down, leaving no arrests.
notable_elements: One of the rare Holmes stories narrated through a third-party client's adventure
A hydraulic press as the murder weapon
The villains escape entirely — another incomplete resolution
A sympathetic female accomplice helps the victim escape

title: The Adventure of the Noble Bachelor
synopsis: Lord St. Simon consults Holmes after his American bride Hatty Doran vanished from their wedding reception immediately after the ceremony. A wedding guest claims to have seen the bride speaking privately with a strange man before she left; her wedding dress is found floating in the Serpentine.
solution: Hatty Doran had secretly married Francis Moulton in America years earlier, believing him dead in a mining accident. When Moulton appeared at the wedding, she recognized him and slipped away to reunite with her lawful husband. Holmes traces the pair and arranges a meeting with Lord St. Simon, who departs indignantly rather than accepting the situation graciously.
notable_elements: A comic tone — Holmes and Watson are amused by the aristocrat's indignation
Holmes's commentary on the difference between American and English attitudes
No crime committed — the mystery resolves through bigamy rather than villainy
Holmes arranges an unconventional dinner-table confrontation

title: The Adventure of the Beryl Coronet
synopsis: Banker Alexander Holder rushes to Baker Street in agitation: he had taken the famous Beryl Coronet as security for a large loan, but found his son Arthur bending it and breaking off three beryls. Arthur refuses to explain himself and is arrested; Holder is desperate to recover the missing stones before the scandal ruins him.
solution: Arthur was trying to wrest the coronet from the actual thief, his cousin Sir George Burnwell, who had seduced his niece Mary into helping him steal it. Arthur's silence was to protect Mary, whom he loved. Holmes recovers the missing beryls by tracking down Burnwell, and Holder's faith in his son is entirely misplaced guilt.
notable_elements: A wrongly accused son protected by his own silence
Holmes purchases the stolen gems back from the criminal
Themes of misplaced trust within a family
Arthur's self-sacrifice for love makes him one of the more sympathetic secondary characters

title: The Adventure of the Copper Beeches
synopsis: Violet Hunter consults Holmes before accepting an unusually well-paid governess post at Copper Beeches near Winchester, where the eccentric employer Mr. Rucastle demands she cut her hair short and wear specific dresses. Holmes senses danger but cannot dissuade her. When she summons him urgently later, he discovers she has been used as a decoy to keep a prisoner hidden.
solution: Rucastle was imprisoning his own daughter Alice, whose fiancé Fowler had been seeking her. He used Violet Hunter — who resembled Alice — to appear at the window and deceive Fowler into thinking Alice was free and uninterested. Holmes and Watson arrive to find Alice has escaped with Fowler, and Rucastle is savagely mauled by his own mastiff.
notable_elements: Closing story of The Adventures — Holmes's dark commentary on the menace of the English countryside
Violet Hunter is one of Holmes's most capable and self-possessed clients
The locked room containing Alice Rucastle
The mastiff as both instrument of terror and poetic retribution

title: Silver Blaze
synopsis: Holmes and Watson travel to Dartmoor to investigate the disappearance of the famous racehorse Silver Blaze before the Wessex Cup and the murder of its trainer John Straker. The horse has vanished and suspicion falls on a bookmaker's tout. Holmes examines the scene and notes the curious incident of the dog in the night-time.
solution: Silver Blaze killed Straker in self-defense: Straker had been attempting to nick the horse's tendon to fix the race, and the horse lashed out and killed him. Holmes recovers Silver Blaze from a neighboring farm where it was hidden. The horse runs and wins the Wessex Cup, concealed in plain sight among the other entries.
notable_elements: Source of the famous exchange: 'the curious incident of the dog in the night-time'
Holmes deduces the killer is the horse itself
Silver Blaze wins the race despite the investigation
The importance of a negative clue — the dog that did not bark

title: The Yellow Face
synopsis: Grant Munro consults Holmes after his wife Effie has been secretive about a cottage near their home in Norbury and has made suspicious withdrawals from her bank account. Holmes deduces that she is hiding something connected to her first marriage in America. The mystery centres on a strange yellow face glimpsed at the cottage window.
solution: Holmes is famously wrong in his deduction. The yellow face belongs to Effie's mixed-race daughter Lucy from her first marriage to John Hebron, an American man of African descent, whom Effie had kept secret from her second husband; Grant Munro, to his credit, accepts the child warmly.
notable_elements: One of the very few cases where Holmes's deduction is entirely wrong
Holmes later asks Watson to remind him of 'Norbury' whenever he grows overconfident
Touches on themes of racial prejudice and mixed-race families in Victorian England
The child's lifelike mask creates the eerie 'yellow face' at the window
A rare example of a case with a fully benign resolution involving no crime

title: The Stockbroker's Clerk
synopsis: Hall Pycroft, a recently-hired stockbroker's clerk, is lured away from his legitimate new post at Mawson & Williams by a mysterious man named Arthur Pinner who offers him a better-paying position and sends him to Birmingham. Holmes suspects fraud when he notices that Pycroft has never actually taken up his post at Mawson's, allowing an impostor to take his place.
solution: The Pinner brothers — one posing as a financial agent, the other having assumed Pycroft's identity at Mawson & Williams — are the forger and cracksman Beddington and his brother; the real Beddington murders the office watchman and attempts to steal nearly a hundred thousand pounds in securities, while his brother attempts suicide upon learning his neck is forfeit.
notable_elements: Identity substitution scheme where a criminal impersonates a legitimate clerk
Holmes deduces the fraud from the inconsistency of Pycroft never reporting to Mawson's
A race-against-time element as Holmes and Watson rush to Birmingham to prevent the robbery
The criminal's brother's attempted suicide adds pathos to an otherwise caper-style crime

title: The Gloria Scott
synopsis: Holmes recounts to Watson the very first case he was ever engaged upon, involving his college friend Victor Trevor and the mysterious terror that a cryptic note inspired in Trevor's father, an otherwise robust country justice of the peace. The story unravels a decades-old secret about a mutiny aboard a convict ship, the Gloria Scott.
solution: Trevor's father was originally a convict transported on the Gloria Scott who survived a mutiny and assumed a new identity in England; Hudson the blackmailer had been a fellow convict, and the cryptic note — read by taking every third word — was a threat that destroyed the old man; he dies of the shock and the secret dies with him.
notable_elements: Holmes's very first case, predating his meeting with Watson
Features a cipher concealed within an apparently innocent letter — every third word forms the real message
A tale of a transported convict who rebuilt his life under a false identity
The Gloria Scott mutiny is a story-within-a-story, told through Trevor's father's confession letter
Establishes Holmes's origins as a consulting detective

title: The Musgrave Ritual
synopsis: Holmes relates another early case to Watson, this one involving Reginald Musgrave and an ancient family catechism called the Musgrave Ritual, which has been recited by Musgraves for generations without anyone understanding its meaning. When the butler Brunton disappears and a maid is found in a state of shock, Holmes deduces that the ritual is in fact a map leading to buried treasure.
solution: Brunton had decoded the ritual and discovered the hiding place of the ancient crown of the Kings of England; he enlisted the maid Rachel Howells to help him retrieve it, but she trapped him in the vault to die and threw the treasure into the lake, later vanishing herself; Holmes recovers the ancient relic.
notable_elements: One of Holmes's early cases before he met Watson
The Musgrave Ritual is a centuries-old catechism that functions as an elaborate treasure map
The recovered treasure is identified as the crown of the Kings of England, hidden during the Civil War
Features Holmes's characteristic method of physically acting out the steps of the ritual
The butler Brunton's death by entombment is one of the more grim resolutions in the Canon

title: The Reigate Squire
synopsis: While recovering from exhaustion at the Surrey home of Watson's friend Colonel Hayter, Holmes is drawn into a local mystery involving a burglary at the house of squire Acton and the subsequent murder of coachman William Kirwan at the Cunningham estate. The torn fragment of a note found clutched in the dead man's hand becomes the key to unravelling the crime.
solution: Old Cunningham and his son Alec are responsible; Kirwan had witnessed the Cunninghams committing the burglary at Acton's house and was blackmailing them, so they murdered him under cover of the widespread burglary scare; Holmes exposes them by analysing the handwriting of the note and provoking them into an attack on him.
notable_elements: Holmes solves the case while supposedly recuperating from nervous exhaustion
Innovative use of handwriting analysis to match the torn note to the Cunninghams
Holmes feigns a physical fit as a ruse to buy time and redirect suspicion
The Cunninghams nearly murder Holmes when he retrieves the incriminating note

title: The Crooked Man
synopsis: Holmes investigates the suspicious death of Colonel James Barclay at Aldershot, whose wife Nancy was found in a state of catalepsy beside his body in a locked room. Mrs. Barclay had been seen arguing violently with her husband the same evening after a chance encounter with a mysterious crippled stranger who had followed her home.
solution: The crippled stranger is Henry Wood, who served with Barclay in India thirty years earlier; Barclay had betrayed Wood to the enemy to win Nancy for himself, and Wood was enslaved and physically ruined; when Barclay saw Wood alive, he died of apoplexy from shock and guilt; the inquest rules the death natural, and Wood goes free.
notable_elements: The locked-room mystery is solved by a window left open — Wood had escaped that way
Features a trained mongoose named Teddy as a plot element
The backstory involves betrayal during the Indian Mutiny
The allusion to the biblical story of David and Uriah the Hittite runs through the tale
Ends with no criminal prosecution — Barclay's own guilty conscience was his punishment

title: The Resident Patient
synopsis: Dr. Percy Trevelyan consults Holmes about his strange arrangement with his financial backer, a reclusive man named Blessington who lives as his resident patient. Blessington has been in a state of terror following a break-in at his rooms, and refuses to explain the cause of his fear. When Blessington is found hanged in his room, Holmes must determine whether it is suicide or murder.
solution: Blessington was really a criminal named Sutton who had turned informer on the Worthingdon bank gang, sending three men to prison and one to the gallows; the three released convicts — Biddle, Hayward, and Moffat — had tracked him down, held a mock trial in his room, and murdered him to avenge their dead comrade.
notable_elements: A murder staged to look like suicide in a locked room
Holmes deduces that multiple visitors entered the room by analysing cigar ash and footprints
The case turns on the concept of criminal vengeance for an informer's betrayal
Blessington's terror is explained only after his death
The three killers are presumed to have perished at sea before justice could reach them

title: The Greek Interpreter
synopsis: Mycroft Holmes, Sherlock's elder brother, brings a case involving a Greek interpreter named Mr. Melas who was blindfolded and taken to a country house where he was forced to interpret for a mysterious prisoner. Melas managed to pass a hidden message to the prisoner, who is being starved and coerced by two criminals into signing away property rights.
solution: Harold Latimer and Wilson Kemp are holding a young Greek man named Paul Kratides prisoner and using his captive sister Sophy to extort his property; Holmes and Mycroft rescue Melas, who is nearly killed with charcoal fumes, though Kratides dies; the villains later perish in Europe, apparently killed by Sophy in revenge.
notable_elements: First introduction of Mycroft Holmes to both Watson and the reader
Mycroft is described as Holmes's intellectual superior, though he lacks the energy to act on his deductions
The Diogenes Club — where members are forbidden to speak — is introduced
Features one of the canon's earliest examples of a newspaper advertisement used as a covert communication
The story ends with the villains receiving poetic justice abroad, hinted to be at Sophy's hands

title: The Naval Treaty
synopsis: Percy Phelps, a Foreign Office clerk and Watson's old schoolfellow, appeals to Holmes after a confidential naval treaty he was charged with copying disappears from his office desk. The theft had caused Phelps to suffer a nine-week nervous breakdown, and he fears the document has fallen into foreign hands with serious consequences for England.
solution: Joseph Harrison, the brother of Phelps's fiancée Annie, stole the treaty opportunistically when he visited the Foreign Office; he concealed it at Phelps's own house in Woking, intending to sell it to a foreign power, but Holmes tracks the hiding place, allows Harrison to retrieve it from concealment, and recovers it, restoring Phelps's career.
notable_elements: The treaty is hidden in plain sight within the room of the victim himself
Holmes uses the ruse of a covered dish at breakfast to dramatically produce the recovered treaty
The theft was a crime of pure opportunism rather than a planned espionage operation
One of the longer stories in the Memoirs, originally published in two parts
Notable for Holmes's famous paean to the beauty of a rose as an example of natural design

title: The Final Problem
synopsis: Holmes reveals he has dismantled Moriarty's criminal network and flees to the Continent as Moriarty pursues him. Near Meiringen, Switzerland, Holmes sends Watson away on a false errand at the Reichenbach Falls. When Watson returns, Holmes is gone — only a farewell note and two sets of footprints leading to the precipice remain.
solution: Holmes and Moriarty struggle at the edge of the Reichenbach Falls and both apparently plunge to their deaths. The true outcome — Holmes survived using Baritsu — is not revealed until The Adventure of the Empty House (book 6).
notable_elements: First introduction of Professor Moriarty as arch-nemesis
Apparent death of Sherlock Holmes — Doyle intended this to end the series
Watson's narration is written as a memorial to Holmes
Introduces Colonel Sebastian Moran
Holmes's farewell letter to Watson

title: The Hound of the Baskervilles
synopsis: Dr. Mortimer brings Holmes the case of Sir Charles Baskerville, found dead on the moor near his estate with an expression of horror on his face. A legend holds that the Baskerville family is cursed by a spectral hound. Holmes sends Watson to accompany the new heir Sir Henry to Dartmoor while secretly conducting his own investigation on the moor.
solution: The hound is real — a large dog painted with phosphorus — and is deployed by Jack Stapleton, a naturalist who is secretly a Baskerville heir seeking to claim the estate. Stapleton starves and controls the hound and drives Sir Charles to a fatal heart attack. Holmes and Watson shoot the hound as it attacks Sir Henry; Stapleton flees into the Grimpen Mire and perishes.
notable_elements: The most celebrated and widely read Sherlock Holmes novel
Watson acts as the primary field investigator while Holmes operates covertly
Set largely on Dartmoor amid the Grimpen Mire
The phosphorus-painted hound — a legendary villain's device
15 chapters — the longest of the Holmes novels

title: The Adventure of the Empty House
synopsis: Three years after Reichenbach, Holmes reappears to Watson, alive. He reveals he survived using Baritsu and spent three years traveling under an alias. The immediate threat is Colonel Moran, responsible for the murder of Ronald Adair. Holmes and Watson trap Moran using a wax bust of Holmes as decoy in the Baker Street window.
solution: Colonel Sebastian Moran is arrested after firing an air gun at a wax bust of Holmes. Holmes explains his three-year disappearance and returns to Baker Street.
notable_elements: Holmes's return after three years
Reveals Holmes survived Reichenbach using Baritsu
Introduces Colonel Sebastian Moran and his custom air gun
The wax bust of Holmes — one of the canon's most theatrical set-pieces

title: The Adventure of the Norwood Builder
synopsis: John Hector McFarlane rushes to Baker Street convinced he is about to be arrested for the murder of Jonas Oldacre, a Norwood builder who had recently made a will in McFarlane's favour. The evidence against McFarlane is overwhelming — blood, a charred mass of remains, and his thumbprint found at the scene — but Holmes suspects a frame-up.
solution: Jonas Oldacre faked his own murder to ruin McFarlane, the son of a woman who had once rejected him, and to defraud his creditors; Oldacre had been hiding in a secret room in his own house, and Holmes exposes him by faking a fire alarm that drives him out of hiding.
notable_elements: Holmes deduces that a fresh thumbprint on the wall was planted after the original investigation
Features Lestrade's premature certainty of McFarlane's guilt contrasted with Holmes's scepticism
Oldacre is one of the more coldly vindictive villains in the Canon
The fake murder-suicide scheme anticipates similar plot devices in later detective fiction

title: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
synopsis: Hilton Cubitt of Ridling Thorpe Manor consults Holmes about mysterious drawings of stick figures that have been appearing on his property, which are causing increasing terror to his American wife Elsie. Holmes recognises the figures as a cipher and sets about decoding them, but tragedy strikes before he can fully intervene.
solution: The messages are from Abe Slaney, an American criminal from Chicago who was formerly betrothed to Elsie and is demanding her return; Holmes cracks the substitution cipher and lures Slaney to the manor, where Slaney had shot Cubitt and Elsie had shot Slaney; Cubitt dies, Slaney is arrested.
notable_elements: Features one of the Canon's most famous cryptographic puzzles — a substitution cipher where each letter is represented by a stick figure
Holmes uses the decoded cipher to send a forged message and capture the criminal
A rare case with a tragic outcome — the client dies before Holmes can save him
The story is set in Norfolk and shows Holmes's skill with American criminal underworld knowledge

title: The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist
synopsis: Violet Smith, a music teacher, consults Holmes about a mysterious man on a bicycle who follows her every Saturday as she rides to and from her remote teaching post at Charlington. Her employer, Mr. Carruthers, and his associate Woodley have both shown an unsettling interest in her, and she fears for her safety.
solution: Woodley and Williamson (a defrocked clergyman posing as a farmer) conspire to kidnap Violet and force her into a sham marriage, intending to claim her inheritance from a South African uncle; Holmes and Watson arrive just in time, Woodley is beaten, and the forced marriage is declared void.
notable_elements: The solitary cyclist following Violet turns out to be Carruthers, watching over her protectively
Features a forced marriage conducted by a defrocked clergyman — a legally invalid ceremony
Holmes sends Watson ahead and is critical of his handling of the preliminary investigation
One of the few stories where a female client is in direct physical danger throughout

title: The Adventure of the Priory School
synopsis: Dr. Thorneycroft Huxtable arrives at Baker Street in a state of collapse to report the disappearance of the young Lord Saltire, son of the Duke of Holdernesse, from the Priory School. A schoolmaster named Heidegger has also vanished, and Holmes must trace the boy across the wild moors of northern England.
solution: James Wilder, the Duke's illegitimate son and secretary, arranged the kidnapping out of jealousy of Lord Saltire's inheritance; the henchman Reuben Hayes murdered Heidegger; Holmes deduces the route of the kidnappers from bicycle tyre tracks and distinctive cow-shaped horseshoes; the Duke pays a large cheque to Holmes for his silence about the family scandal.
notable_elements: Holmes tracks the route using bicycle tyre marks across open moorland
Includes the famous detail of horses shod with iron shoes shaped like cow hooves to disguise their tracks
Holmes accepts a cheque from the Duke rather than press the full legal consequences
The Duke's complicity in concealing the kidnapper's identity is a morally ambiguous element

title: The Adventure of Black Peter
synopsis: Captain Peter Carey, known as Black Peter for his violent temper, is found harpooned through the chest in his isolated outhouse in Woodman's Lee. A young stockbroker named John Neligan is found lurking near the scene, apparently connected to a missing notebook, and is arrested. Holmes works the case in disguise under the alias Captain Basil.
solution: Patrick Cairns, a harpooner who had served on the Sea Unicorn with Black Peter, killed Carey when Carey refused to share the proceeds of a blackmail plot over the Sea Unicorn's log; Holmes baits a trap by advertising for harpooners seeking Arctic employment, luring Cairns to Baker Street where he is arrested.
notable_elements: Holmes tests whether a man could drive a harpoon through a pig's carcass to establish the strength required
Holmes operates in disguise as Captain Basil for days before Watson realises
The Sea Unicorn log documents a murder and insurance fraud Carey had been profiting from
Neligan is cleared — he was searching for his father's bonds that Black Peter had stolen years earlier

title: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton
synopsis: Charles Augustus Milverton, described by Holmes as the worst man in London, is a blackmailer who holds compromising letters belonging to a young noblewoman. When negotiations fail and Holmes cannot recover the letters by legitimate means, he and Watson disguise themselves and burgle Milverton's house — only to witness an unexpected killing.
solution: While Holmes and Watson hide in Milverton's study during their burglary, a woman whose husband was ruined by Milverton arrives and shoots him dead; Holmes refuses to assist the police investigation, considering Milverton's death a justified act of private vengeance; the woman's identity is hinted at by her resemblance to a famous noblewoman's portrait.
notable_elements: Holmes and Watson commit an actual burglary — one of the most morally complex acts in the Canon
Holmes becomes secretly engaged to Milverton's housemaid to gather intelligence, which Watson finds distasteful
The true killer is never named — Holmes refuses to hand her to the police
Holmes describes Milverton as worse than any murderer, as blackmailers cause indirect deaths

title: The Adventure of the Six Napoleons
synopsis: Lestrade reports a bizarre case of someone systematically seeking out and smashing plaster busts of Napoleon across London, a crime initially attributed to madness. When the crime escalates to murder — a man found stabbed beside a broken bust — Holmes realises the vandal has a specific purpose and traces the busts to their source.
solution: An Italian criminal named Beppo hid the famous Borgia black pearl inside one of six Napoleon busts while they were still drying at the factory; he was imprisoned before he could retrieve it, and upon release he tracked down each bust to find the right one; the murdered man was his confederate who held him responsible for the lost pearl.
notable_elements: Features Lestrade's rare public tribute to Holmes at the story's end
The Borgia black pearl is a famous gem of historical significance used as a MacGuffin
Holmes narrows down which bust contains the pearl by systematic elimination
One of the most celebrated short stories in the Canon for its logical structure

title: The Adventure of the Three Students
synopsis: Hilton Soames, a lecturer at St. Luke's College in an unnamed university town, consults Holmes after discovering that someone has broken into his study and copied part of the forthcoming examination paper for the Fortescue Scholarship. Only three students had opportunity, and Holmes must identify the culprit before the examination takes place.
solution: Gilchrist, the most athletic and capable of the three students, broke in and copied the paper; he was discovered because he had tracked in clay from the long-jump pit on Soames's floor, and left a distinctive scratch on a chair when jumping out; overcome with remorse, he confesses and withdraws from the examination voluntarily.
notable_elements: A case involving academic dishonesty rather than violent crime
Holmes deduces the culprit from a small pellet of clay and a scratch on a chair
The college setting is deliberately kept vague to avoid identifying real institutions
One of only a few stories set outside London or its immediate environs

title: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez
synopsis: Inspector Stanley Hopkins summons Holmes to Yoxley Old Place in Kent, where young secretary Willoughby Smith has been found stabbed to death. The only clue is a pair of woman's golden pince-nez spectacles clutched in the victim's hand. The professor who employs him has been shut in his study all morning, but the professor's room shows signs of a recent visitor.
solution: Professor Coram's room conceals his Russian wife, whom he had betrayed to the police years ago in St. Petersburg, causing her to be sent to Siberia; she had escaped and traced him, and Willoughby Smith died accidentally when she stabbed him after he caught her stealing papers; she takes poison before being arrested.
notable_elements: Holmes constructs a physical description of the unknown woman from the pince-nez alone
The hidden room within the professor's study is a clever concealment device
A backstory of political betrayal in Tsarist Russia provides the motive
The woman's death by her own hand before arrest gives the story a tragic resolution

title: The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter
synopsis: Cyril Overton, captain of the Cambridge rugby team, arrives at Baker Street in a panic: Godfrey Staunton, their star three-quarter back, has vanished the night before an important varsity match. Holmes traces Staunton to a remote cottage in the Cambridgeshire countryside, where a physician named Dr. Armstrong has been obstinately shielding him.
solution: Staunton had secretly married his landlady's daughter, concealing the marriage to protect his inheritance from a miserly uncle; his wife was dying of consumption and he had rushed to her bedside; no crime had been committed, and Holmes protects the secret from public exposure.
notable_elements: A rare case with no crime — the mystery turns out to be a personal secret kept out of financial necessity
Dr. Armstrong's fierce loyalty to his patient creates an adversarial dynamic with Holmes
Holmes tracks Staunton by spraying aniseed on a carriage wheel and following a dog's nose
Features Holmes's candid admission that guarding secrets is sometimes more important than exposing them

title: The Adventure of the Abbey Grange
synopsis: Inspector Hopkins summons Holmes to Abbey Grange in Kent, where the brutal Sir Eustace Brackenstall has been found battered to death and his wife Lady Mary tied to a chair. Lady Mary blames the Randall gang of burglars who came for her silver, but Holmes finds inconsistencies in her account — particularly the condition of the wine glasses.
solution: Captain Crocker, a sailor who loved Lady Mary and could not bear her abuse at the hands of her alcoholic husband, had visited the house and killed Sir Eustace in a fight; Lady Mary and Crocker staged the burglary scene; Holmes acts as a one-man jury and acquits Crocker, advising him to return in a year to claim Lady Mary.
notable_elements: Holmes notices that two of the three wine glasses have been drunk from, contradicting the story of three burglars
Features Holmes famously convening Watson as a jury and pronouncing a verdict outside the law
The victim Sir Eustace is presented as deserving of little sympathy — a brutal and drunken husband
Holmes's willingness to let a killer go free makes this one of the canon's most ethically complex resolutions

title: The Adventure of the Second Stain
synopsis: The Prime Minister and the Secretary for European Affairs consult Holmes in the utmost secrecy: a sensitive diplomatic letter has been stolen from a locked despatch-box, and its publication could provoke a war. The spy Eduardo Lucas is murdered the same night, leading Holmes to investigate both crimes simultaneously.
solution: Lady Hilda Trelawney Hope, the Secretary's wife, stole the letter under blackmail by Lucas, who held an incriminating letter from her past; after Lucas was killed by his wife, Lady Hilda secretly recovered the diplomatic letter from his room and hid it under her drawing-room carpet; Holmes confronts her and replaces the letter before her husband discovers the theft.
notable_elements: One of the Canon's great international espionage cases involving a threat of war
Watson notes this was originally considered too sensitive to publish
The second stain on the carpet shows where the letter was hidden, giving the case its title
Holmes protects Lady Hilda's secret to preserve her marriage, a quietly compassionate act
Intended by Watson as a fitting final adventure for the Return collection

title: The Valley of Fear
synopsis: Holmes receives a coded warning from a Moriarty agent and investigates the apparent murder of John Douglas at Birlstone Manor — the victim found shot and disfigured beyond recognition. Part Two reveals Douglas's American past as Birdy Edwards, a Pinkerton detective who infiltrated the violent Scowrers gang in the Pennsylvania coalfields and faked his own death to escape them.
solution: John Douglas is not dead — he killed his would-be assassin Ted Baldwin, substituted the body, and hid in the manor. Holmes deduces the substitution but allows Douglas to disappear. The epilogue reveals Douglas was later tracked down by Moriarty's agents and killed at sea, and that Moriarty was behind the Scowrers all along.
notable_elements: Two-part structure: Part I in England, Part II in Pennsylvania coal country
Moriarty mentioned as the spider behind the plot, though never appearing directly
Based partly on the real Molly Maguires labor conflicts
Holmes solves the case but cannot save Douglas in the end
The last Holmes novel Doyle completed

title: The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge
synopsis: John Scott Eccles consults Holmes after a bizarre experience: he spent the night at Wisteria Lodge near Esher at the invitation of an acquaintance named Garcia, only to wake and find the house completely deserted. Holmes links this to the discovery of Garcia's murdered body on Oxshott Common, and Inspector Baynes of the Surrey police becomes an unexpectedly capable collaborator.
solution: Garcia was plotting revenge against Don Murillo, the Tiger of San Pedro — a deposed Central American dictator living in England under a false identity; Garcia was killed by Murillo's agents before he could strike; Holmes identifies Murillo at High Gable, but the villain escapes to Spain where he is eventually murdered.
notable_elements: Features a gruesome voodoo kitchen with animal sacrifices that provide key evidence
Inspector Baynes is one of the few police officers portrayed as a genuine peer to Holmes
Holmes admits the case is chaotic and unusually difficult to wrap into a neat solution
The story is set partly in the Caribbean and South American political backstory

title: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
synopsis: Miss Susan Cushing of Croydon receives a cardboard box in the post containing two severed human ears packed in salt. Inspector Lestrade suspects it is a medical students' prank directed at her from some other address, but Holmes finds the packaging and the ears themselves evidence of a more sinister crime.
solution: Jim Browner, a sailor and Susan's brother-in-law, murdered his wife Mary and her lover Fairbairn in a fit of jealous rage during a boat trip on the Mersey, sinking the bodies and sending the ears to Susan, whom he blamed for engineering the affair; Browner confesses fully and is arrested.
notable_elements: Contains one of Holmes's most famous demonstrations of reading Watson's unspoken thoughts
The case turns on Holmes's observation that two ears were cut from different heads — left and right — ruling out a single victim
Holmes traces the string and packaging to establish the box's origin
Originally suppressed from certain editions of the Memoirs due to its violent subject matter

title: The Adventure of the Red Circle
synopsis: Mrs. Warren, a London landlady, consults Holmes about a lodger who has paid for a fortnight's board but remains entirely unseen, communicating only by written messages slipped under the door. Holmes realises the lodger is communicating in cipher using newspaper agony column advertisements.
solution: The mysterious lodger is Gennaro Lucca, an Italian man whose wife Emilia is disguised as the lodger to hide her husband from the Red Circle, a Neapolitan crime society; the story ends with Holmes and Inspector Gregson witnessing the Red Circle's agent Gorgiano being killed by Gennaro in self-defence.
notable_elements: Features communication through newspaper agony column messages in cipher
The Red Circle is a Neapolitan criminal organisation that pursues its members across continents
Holmes uses semaphore-style candle signals to communicate with the lodger
Inspector Gregson of Scotland Yard appears as a capable officer familiar with the Italian criminal underground

title: The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans
synopsis: During a dense November fog, Mycroft Holmes arrives at Baker Street to ask his brother to investigate the disappearance of the Bruce-Partington submarine plans, seven of whose ten pages have been found on the body of young Arthur Cadogan West, whose corpse was discovered on the Underground railway. The case has grave implications for national security.
solution: Colonel Valentine Walter, brother of the plan's custodian, stole the plans and sold them to the spy Hugo Oberstein; Cadogan West had been murdered and his body placed atop a train using the roof of an Underground signal-box as a means of disposal; Holmes lures Oberstein back to England with a forged advertisement and he is arrested with the plans.
notable_elements: Mycroft's second appearance, and the first time he leaves his habitual route between his lodgings and the Diogenes Club
Holmes deduces the body was placed on top of a moving train by the presence of no blood on the line
Features Mycroft acknowledging his brother is essential where direct action is required
Holmes receives a gift from Queen Victoria for his service — a remarkably fine emerald tie-pin

title: The Adventure of the Dying Detective
synopsis: Mrs. Hudson fetches Watson because Holmes appears to be dying of a rare Asiatic disease contracted during a case in Rotherhithe. Holmes seems delirious, refuses Watson's medical attention, and insists on summoning a specialist named Culverton Smith. Watson is mystified by Holmes's erratic behaviour and curious instructions.
solution: Holmes has been feigning illness to lure Culverton Smith, who had murdered his nephew Victor Savage with a poisoned device concealed in a box; Holmes tricks Smith into a confession by allowing him to believe Holmes is dying, while Watson and Inspector Morton are hidden in the room to make the arrest.
notable_elements: Holmes fasts for three days and uses cosmetics — belladonna, rouge, and beeswax — to simulate a dying man
He deliberately keeps Watson ignorant because Watson cannot dissimulate convincingly
The trap springs when Smith gloats over Holmes's presumed dying state and confesses
Features detailed description of Holmes's Baker Street domestic habits and Mrs. Hudson's loyalty

title: The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax
synopsis: Holmes sends Watson to Lausanne to investigate the disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax, an English noblewoman and regular traveller who has inexplicably cut off communication with her friends. Watson traces her movements through Switzerland and southern France, discovering she had recently come under the influence of a sinister couple named Holy Peters and his wife.
solution: Holy Peters, a fraudster posing as a missionary, had befriended Lady Frances to steal her jewellery, and had her drugged and concealed inside an oversized coffin ordered ostensibly for a dead missionary woman; Holmes realises the deception when he notices the coffin is far larger than necessary and arrives just in time to prevent Lady Frances from being buried alive.
notable_elements: One of Holmes's closest near-failures — Lady Frances is very nearly buried alive
Watson conducts the initial investigation independently and is sent abroad — a rare extended solo assignment
Holmes criticises his own reasoning at the end for taking too long to identify the coffin scheme
Features the disturbing device of hiding a living victim inside a coffin alongside a corpse

title: The Adventure of the Devil's Foot
synopsis: While Holmes is recuperating in Cornwall on doctor's orders, he is drawn into a local tragedy: the Tregennis family is found in their sitting room in a state of madness or death, having apparently been terrified into insanity overnight. The only substance found at the scene is the remains of some burned powder in the lamp.
solution: Mortimer Tregennis poisoned his siblings using devil's-foot root, a rare West African drug that induces terror and madness, obtained from the explorer Dr. Leon Sterndale; Tregennis wanted to gain sole control of the family property; Sterndale in turn executes Tregennis using the same poison in revenge for the death of Brenda, the woman he loved, and Holmes lets him go.
notable_elements: Holmes and Watson nearly kill themselves when Holmes experiments with the devil's-foot powder
The Cornish setting — remote, atmospheric, with prehistoric standing stones — is atmospheric and unusual
Holmes allows a killer to go free for the second time in the Canon, citing the justice of the revenge
Features one of Holmes's most candid moments: he implies he would have done the same in Sterndale's place

title: His Last Bow
synopsis: On the night of 2 August 1914 — the eve of England's entry into the First World War — the German master spy Von Bork is preparing to flee England with years of accumulated intelligence. He awaits his last agent, an Irish-American named Altamont, who arrives with a final prize: the naval signal codes.
solution: Altamont is Sherlock Holmes in disguise, who has spent two years in deep cover penetrating German naval intelligence; Watson is also present; Holmes drugs Von Bork and turns him over to Watson, leaving with a philosophical reflection on the coming storm and England's eventual triumph.
notable_elements: The only Holmes story told in the third person, not narrated by Watson
Set on the eve of World War One — 2 August 1914 — making it the chronologically latest canonical case
Holmes has been in deep undercover work for two years, posing as an Irish-American spy
Holmes's closing speech about the 'east wind' is one of the most quoted passages in the Canon
Reveals that Holmes had retired to the Sussex Downs to keep bees before being recalled for the war effort

title: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
synopsis: A mysterious client acting through an intermediary, Sir James Damery, asks Holmes to prevent the marriage of an English noblewoman, Violet de Merville, to the notorious Austrian adventurer Baron Adelbert Gruner, known to have murdered a previous wife. Holmes must find a way to open Violet's eyes to Gruner's true nature before she marries him.
solution: Holmes obtains Gruner's personal diary of his conquests from his Chinese pottery collection with the help of a woman named Kitty Winter, after Holmes is badly beaten by Gruner's thugs; Gruner is disfigured by Kitty Winter who throws vitriol at him; the diary is shown to Violet, breaking her infatuation; Holmes's illustrious client is revealed to be the King himself.
notable_elements: Holmes is seriously assaulted and hospitalised during the investigation
Features Kitty Winter, one of Gruner's discarded victims, as a key accomplice
The illustrious client is deliberately kept unnamed, though Watson glimpses a royal crest
Holmes uses a decoy conversation about Chinese pottery to give himself time to steal the diary

title: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
synopsis: James M. Dodd consults Holmes about his friend Godfrey Emsworth, a fellow soldier he served with in the Boer War, who has mysteriously vanished from his family's estate at Tuxbury Old Park. When Dodd visits the estate, he catches a glimpse of a ghostly pale figure — apparently Godfrey — but the family refuses to explain his whereabouts or condition.
solution: Godfrey contracted a skin disease resembling leprosy while in South Africa and has been kept in isolation by his family out of shame and fear; a specialist confirms it is not true leprosy but a closely related condition, ichthyosis, which is treatable, resolving the situation.
notable_elements: One of only two stories narrated by Holmes himself rather than Watson
Holmes opens by praising Watson's usefulness as a sounding board even while gently mocking his literary style
The story hinges on the fear and stigma of leprosy in Edwardian England
Features a medical resolution rather than criminal — no villain, only a frightened family

title: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
synopsis: The Crown diamond — known as the Mazarin Stone — has been stolen, and Holmes has been tasked with recovering it. The prime suspect is Count Negretto Sylvius, a dangerous criminal, and Holmes draws him and his confederate Sam Merton into a trap at Baker Street, using a wax dummy and a gramophone to create a convincing decoy.
solution: Holmes tricks Count Sylvius into revealing the hiding place of the Mazarin Stone — which had been concealed in Sylvius's own hat — by using a hidden gramophone playing violin music to make Sylvius believe Holmes is busy; Holmes slips the recovered diamond into Lord Cantlemere's pocket as a prank before revealing the recovery.
notable_elements: Written in the third person, like His Last Bow, with Watson appearing as a visitor rather than narrator
Features the wax bust of Holmes (artifact_wax-bust-of-holmes) as a decoy
The gramophone playing violin music is used to create an alibi for Holmes's movements
Lord Cantlemere finds the diamond in his own pocket in one of Holmes's characteristically theatrical reveals

title: The Adventure of the Three Gables
synopsis: An elderly widow, Mrs. Maberley, has received an extraordinary offer to buy her house at Three Gables in Harrow, complete with all its contents. Holmes is also warned off the case by a threatening visit from a bruiser named Steve Dixie. Holmes connects the affair to a wealthy socialite, Isadora Klein, and a manuscript written by her late lover.
solution: Isadora Klein arranged for Mrs. Maberley's house to be acquired — and her belongings to be stolen — to suppress a compromising manuscript written by her deceased young lover Douglas Maberley, who had recorded their affair; Holmes forces Klein to pay five thousand pounds to Mrs. Maberley in compensation.
notable_elements: Holmes is threatened in his own rooms at the outset, which he dismisses with characteristic coolness
Features Holmes compelling a financial settlement rather than a criminal prosecution
Isadora Klein is one of the few antagonists to escape legal consequences entirely
The manuscript as a dangerous document echoes the theme of Milverton's compromising letters

title: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire
synopsis: Robert Ferguson writes to Holmes in distress: his Peruvian wife has been observed sucking blood from their infant child's neck. The servants believe she is a vampire. Ferguson's teenage son from a first marriage, Jacky, is a cripple who seems to bear the new wife a mysterious hatred.
solution: Jacky, the stepson, had been poisoning the baby with a dart from a Peruvian bow; the wife had seen him and was sucking out the poison to save the child's life, not harming it; she said nothing to protect her husband from the knowledge of his son's attempted murder.
notable_elements: Holmes dismisses the supernatural explanation from the outset — 'No ghosts need apply'
Features the reference to 'the giant rat of Sumatra, a story for which the world is not yet prepared'
The true solution is revealed to be an act of maternal self-sacrifice under a false accusation
One of the most famous examples of Holmes explaining away an apparently supernatural occurrence

title: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs
synopsis: A man calling himself John Garrideb of Kansas arrives to enlist Holmes in finding a third person with the surname Garrideb, claiming a peculiar bequest requires three Garridebs to share a fortune. Holmes is immediately suspicious of the story, but allows the scheme to play out in order to discover what Garrideb actually wants.
solution: John Garrideb is actually a wanted American criminal named Killer Evans; the entire Garrideb scheme was a ruse to get Nathan Garrideb out of his flat so Evans could retrieve a counterfeiting press hidden in the floor by a previous occupant; Evans shoots Watson when caught, prompting one of Holmes's most emotional moments.
notable_elements: Contains one of Holmes's most famous emotional reactions — he shows genuine distress when Watson is shot
Watson notes that Holmes refused a knighthood the same month
The Garrideb story is an elaborate fraud designed merely to empty a single flat
The counterfeiting press hidden beneath the floorboards is the actual goal of the entire operation

title: The Problem of Thor Bridge
synopsis: Neil Gibson, the Gold King and former American senator, asks Holmes to clear his governess Miss Grace Dunbar, who is charged with the murder of Gibson's wife Maria on Thor Bridge. The evidence against Miss Dunbar is strong — she was found holding a note arranging a meeting at the bridge — but Holmes finds a chip in the stone parapet that changes everything.
solution: Maria Gibson, consumed by obsessive jealousy, murdered herself in a way designed to frame Miss Dunbar; she tied one revolver to a stone and dropped it over the bridge, so it would snap back after firing the fatal shot, then held a note from Miss Dunbar in her hand; Holmes recovers the weighted gun from the lake to prove it.
notable_elements: One of the most ingenious suicide-as-murder-frame schemes in the Canon
Holmes identifies the crime by noticing a chip in the bridge parapet where the weighted string had been attached
Opening pages list several unsolved cases from Watson's dispatch box, including the famous Phillimore mystery
Features Holmes's reflection on the danger of assuming guilt from circumstantial evidence

title: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
synopsis: Professor Presbury, a distinguished biologist at a university, has been behaving strangely since becoming engaged to a woman half his age — prowling at night, climbing the ivy on his house, and attacking his own dog. His assistant Bennett brings the case to Holmes, who traces a mysterious correspondence from Prague.
solution: Professor Presbury has been injecting himself with a serum derived from langur monkeys — obtained from a Prague scientist named Lowenstein — in an attempt to restore his youth and vitality for his young fiancee; the injections are causing him to temporarily revert to ape-like behaviour.
notable_elements: One of the most science-fiction-adjacent stories in the Canon, anticipating themes of body modification
Holmes links the case to his monograph on the uses of dogs — his dog Roy's behaviour was the first clue
The story is set shortly before Holmes's retirement from practice
Raises ethical questions about the limits of science in the pursuit of youth and love

title: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
synopsis: Holmes, now retired to the Sussex Downs and keeping bees, witnesses the death of science master Fitzroy McPherson, who staggers to the top of a cliff crying 'Lion's Mane' before dying of mysterious whip-like wounds. When a second man is found similarly injured, Holmes must solve the case without Watson's assistance.
solution: The killer is Cyanea capillata — the lion's mane jellyfish — whose tentacles leave precisely the pattern of welts found on the victims; McPherson and the dog that also died had both swum in a pool where a large specimen was lurking; the jellyfish is found and destroyed in the pool.
notable_elements: The second story narrated by Holmes himself (with Watson absent in retirement)
Set after Holmes has retired to the Sussex Downs — one of the latest-set stories in the Canon
Features a non-human, non-criminal 'culprit' — a rare example of a fatal natural cause
Holmes blames himself for not recognising the clue sooner, despite its obscure nature

title: The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger
synopsis: Mrs. Merrilow informs Holmes that her veiled lodger, a woman who conceals her terribly disfigured face, wishes to confess a secret she has kept for seven years. The woman, Mrs. Ronder, was the wife of a circus proprietor and survived a lion attack that killed her husband under circumstances she says were not quite what they appeared.
solution: Mrs. Ronder and her lover Abbas Parva contrived the death of her brutal husband using a trained lion; she was herself mauled when the plan went wrong; her lover later committed suicide; wracked by guilt for seven years, she confesses to Holmes and sends him the means of her own suicide, but ultimately chooses to live when Holmes counsels patience.
notable_elements: One of the shortest and most melancholy stories in the Canon
No active investigation — Holmes simply listens to a woman's confession
Holmes talks a woman back from suicide with a quiet appeal to endurance
Watson opens the story with a warning that someone has been attempting to steal Holmes's case files

title: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
synopsis: Head trainer John Mason consults Holmes about the strange behaviour of his employer Sir Robert Norberton at Shoscombe Old Place: the squire has been sending away his sister's beloved spaniels, acting erratically, and meeting secretly at night in a crypt below the old church. Watson, a racing man, provides background on Sir Robert's desperate financial situation.
solution: Lady Beatrice Falder, Sir Robert's sister, died a week before a critical race on which Sir Robert had bet his entire fortune; if her death were known, his creditors would have seized the estate and prevented the horse Shoscombe Prince from running; Sir Robert was concealing the body in an old coffin in the crypt and using a hired woman to impersonate his sister until after the race.
notable_elements: Often cited as the last Holmes story written by Conan Doyle
Unusual in that Watson's knowledge of horse-racing provides crucial background
The concealment of a dead body as a financial stratagem is a macabre solution to a desperate situation
Shoscombe Prince wins the Derby and Sir Robert repays his creditors, earning a relatively lenient legal outcome

title: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
synopsis: Josiah Amberley, a retired paint-box manufacturer, tells Holmes that his much younger wife and his chess-playing friend Dr. Ray Ernest have run off together, taking his life savings. Holmes sends Watson to Amberley's house while he pursues another angle, and the subsequent investigation reveals a far darker truth beneath the apparent case of a faithless wife.
solution: Amberley murdered both his wife and Dr. Ernest by locking them in a secret strong-room and flooding it with gas from a concealed pipe; he had invented the story of their elopement to conceal the murders; Holmes proves the murders by finding a message scratched on the wall by one of the dying victims.
notable_elements: Holmes deliberately sends Watson on a distracting errand to test a theory without warning him
The strong-room disguised as a hobby space is a chillingly mundane murder weapon
Features Barker, a rival private detective, who independently arrives at the same conclusion as Holmes
Holmes's opening meditation on the pathos of human existence gives the story an unusually philosophical tone
